sections.title: Step 09 — Technology-Level Feature-Market Fit
sections.content:
**Technology class:** Emergency Exit Security Devices with Remote Monitoring
**Commodity FP:** Prevent unauthorized use of emergency exit doors while maintaining unimpeded escape capability and alerting to door activation via audible alarm
**Market top needs (OS ≥ 12):** E6 unauthorized exit undetected (OS 14) · E9 centralized monitoring (OS 14) · E10 alert latency <30 sec (OS 13) · E15 digital audit log (OS 13) · E18 floor consistency (OS 12) · E7 false alert fatigue (OS 12) · E19 inspection compliance (OS 12)

### Kano + ODI Scores

| Feature | Kano | Time | Cost | Safety | Reliability | Skill | Stress | Pain | Confidence | Delight | **Overall** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Mechanical Handle Locking | `must_be` | 2 | 3 | 9 | 8 | 6 | 7 | 5 | 8 | 2 | **5.56** |
| Audible Alarm Trigger | `must_be` | 4 | 3 | 9 | 7 | 5 | 5 | 6 | 7 | 2 | **5.33** |
| Single-Motion Emergency Release | `must_be` | 5 | 2 | 10 | 8 | 8 | 8 | 3 | 9 | 3 | **6.22** |
| Key Override Bypass | `must_be` | 6 | 5 | 5 | 6 | 5 | 7 | 6 | 7 | 3 | **5.56** |
| Battery-Powered Operation | `attractive` | 7 | 8 | 4 | 6 | 7 | 6 | 8 | 5 | 7 | **6.44** |
| Pre-Alarm Warning | `attractive` | 3 | 4 | 3 | 5 | 5 | 7 | 6 | 5 | 6 | **4.89** |

`overall_fit = (Time + Cost + Safety + Reliability + Skill + Stress + Pain + Confidence + Delight) / 9`

### Kano Rationale

| Feature | Kano class | Primary drivers | Market evidence |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mechanical Handle Locking | `must_be` | Safety (9): physically blocks unauthorized door operation — the commodity baseline. Reliability (8): zero-power operation ensures function during hotel power events or fire. | E8 (imp=10, sat=8): escape non-impedance is the baseline expectation; every alternative product delivers it. |
| Audible Alarm Trigger | `must_be` | Safety (9): immediate deterrence of propping/misuse. Reliability (7): mechanical piezo trigger needs no power, confirmed by C2 (absolute constraint). Stress (5) deliberately lower: 95 dB alarm on upper floors disturbs guests — reduces experiential score. | E6 (imp=9, sat=4): detecting unauthorized open events is the core need; alarm is the lowest-tech expression of this. |
| Single-Motion Emergency Release | `must_be` | Safety (10): direct EN 179/1125 evacuation compliance — non-negotiable for any certified product. Skill (8) + Stress (8): unfamiliar hotel guests can escape without prior knowledge per DIN EN 1125 mandate. | E8 (imp=10, sat=8): only satisfied need in the top OS cluster; every certified product delivers this. Confidence (9): operators have documented compliance evidence. |
| Key Override Bypass | `must_be` | Stress (7): housekeeping, maintenance, and night audit staff can access silently. Time (6) + Pain (6): eliminates alarm trigger + investigation cycle for routine authorized access. | E7 (imp=8, sat=4): false alert fatigue from authorized staff uses is a top-8 underserved need; key override is the primary mitigation at hardware level. |
| Battery-Powered Operation | `attractive` | Cost (8): eliminates mains wiring conduit — the dominant retrofit cost driver in hotels (S3 mid-scale). Pain (8): removes need for electrical permits, conduit work, and structural disruption. Time (7): enables sub-weekend deployment per door (O4 in outcomes). Delight (7): operators are genuinely surprised that IoT monitoring works without rewiring. | E3 (imp=8, sat=5, product_related=true): commissioning speed is a top-10 underserved need; battery power is the enabler. Not expected — wired (A2) is the traditional paradigm. |
| Pre-Alarm Warning | `attractive` | Stress (7): reduces guest disturbance from accidental alarm triggers (housekeeping leaning on door). Pain (6): eliminates investigation burden from handle-touch incidents. Delight (6): unexpected feature that specifically addresses hotel's guest experience sensitivity. | E7 (imp=8, sat=4): false alert reduction is underserved; pre-alarm provides hardware-level filtering before IoT alert layer. Absent from commodity FP and basic variants — above baseline. |

---

sections.title: Step 10 — Product-Related Needs (Burleson Product Jobs)
sections.content:
Litmus test applied: each need would disappear if a wired panic bar (A2) or guard patrol (A4) were chosen instead.

| # | Statement | Source Constraint | Imp | Sat | Affected |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| N1 | Minimize the likelihood of incomplete wireless coverage from 30 m transmitter range leaving emergency exits on floors above level 3 without active IoT monitoring | Wireless Range Limitation | 7 | 3 | hotels_motels |
| N2 | Minimize the time required to design and validate wireless receiver placement to achieve >95% floor coverage before hardware procurement in multi-story hotel installations | Wireless Range Limitation | 6 | 3 | hotels_motels |
| N3 | Minimize the likelihood of 9V battery depletion in high-traffic hotel emergency exits exhausting monitoring capability between scheduled maintenance cycles while mechanical alarm remains functional | IoT Battery Life Floor | 7 | 4 | hotels_motels |
| N4 | Minimize the time required to identify and replace batteries approaching depletion threshold across a 30+ device hotel deployment before wireless monitoring capability is lost | IoT Battery Life Floor | 6 | 4 | hotels_motels |
| N5 | Minimize the likelihood of surface-mounted door guardian housing conflicting with hotel brand interior finish specifications in guest-facing corridors and stairwells | Retrofit-Only Surface Mount | 5 | 4 | hotels_motels (S2 upscale) |
| N6 | Minimize the effort required to bridge EasyWave event data to hotel BMS platforms running BACnet, KNX, or IP-based protocols without deploying a dedicated GfS gateway per property | EasyWave Protocol Lock-In | 7 | 4 | hotels_motels (S1, S2) |
| N7 | Minimize the likelihood of proprietary wireless protocol dependency preventing multi-brand emergency exit monitoring consolidation in hotel portfolio standardization programs | EasyWave Protocol Lock-In | 6 | 4 | hotels_motels (S1 chains) |
| N8 | Minimize the likelihood of door-event timestamp and access log data triggering a GDPR Data Protection Impact Assessment that delays IoT system deployment in EU hotel properties | GDPR Monitoring Data Compliance | 6 | 5 | hotels_motels (EU) |

**Needs by constraint:** Wireless Range (2) · Battery Life (2) · Surface Mount (1) · Protocol Lock-In (2) · GDPR (1) = 8 total

**Litmus verification:**
- N1–N2: wired system has no RF range limitation → need disappears ✓
- N3–N4: guard patrol has no battery → need disappears ✓
- N5: flush-integrated wired system has no visible protrusion → need disappears ✓
- N6–N7: wired BACnet system natively integrates with BMS → need disappears ✓
- N8: purely mechanical system logs nothing → GDPR need disappears ✓

---

sections.title: Step 11 — Vendor Feature-Market Fit (GfS Türwächter)
sections.content:
**Vendor:** GfS Rettungswegtechnik (Assa Abloy Group)
**Product:** Exit Control 179/1125 with IoT (Pre-alarm + Radio variant, Art. 990140)
**Best incumbents per feature:** dormakaba SafeRoute (LON/BMS integrated) · GEZE SecuLogic (IP-based) · Allegion/Von Duprin (electrified panic hardware)

Advantage rule: superior = vendor > incumbent by >1 pt · parity = |gap| ≤ 1 · inferior = vendor < incumbent by >1 pt

| Vendor Feature | Market | Kano | Vendor | Incumbent | Incumbent Ref | Advantage | Rationale |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 868 MHz EasyWave Radio Transmission | hotels_motels | `one_dimensional` | 7 | 5 | Generic wireless door alarm / Allegion wireless | **superior** | GfS's 868 MHz EU-reserved band provides reliable low-interference transmission in dense hotel RF environments; purpose-built for exit alarm state events. Allegion/Von Duprin wireless monitoring uses higher-frequency BLE/Zigbee with shorter effective range and more interference sensitivity in concrete structures. Constraint C3 (30 m) limits multi-floor hotels but is addressable with repeaters unlike competitors that require per-floor wired drops. |
| Wireless BUS Interface Integration | hotels_motels | `attractive` | 6 | 8 | dormakaba SafeRoute (LON bus) · GEZE SecuLogic (BACnet/IP) | **inferior** | dormakaba SafeRoute natively integrates with LON bus networks and provides centralized software dashboards with open BMS protocols. GEZE SecuLogic supports BACnet/IP directly. GfS BUS Interface is proprietary — hotels with existing BMS (S1 chains, S2 upscale) require additional GfS gateway hardware for protocol translation, adding integration cost and vendor dependency (C6). Full-service hotel chains explicitly require BACnet or KNX native support. |
| Dual-Standard Variant Range (EN 179 + EN 1125) | hotels_motels | `one_dimensional` | 8 | 6 | dormakaba SafeRoute · GEZE SecuLogic | **superior** | GfS offers matched lever-handle (EN 179, Art. 990140) and panic-bar (EN 1125, Art. 994100) variants from the same product family with shared wireless infrastructure. Hotels have mixed hardware: stairwell ground-floor exits typically require EN 1125 (panic bar per public building code); upper-floor corridor exits use EN 179 lever. dormakaba and GEZE cover both standards but via separate product lines at higher cost/complexity, preventing single-vendor deployment with unified monitoring. |
| IP45 Outdoor Variant | hotels_motels | `attractive` | 7 | 5 | Standard mechanical alarm guards (no published IP rating) | **superior** | GfS IP45 outdoor variant (Art. 990000-IP) enables monitoring of hotel perimeter exits — loading docks, external stairwells, covered parking exits — without a separate product selection. Best mechanical incumbent alternatives (40% of hotel deployments) carry no published IP rating, defaulting to indoor-only installation and excluding outdoor exits from monitoring coverage. Hotels with mixed indoor/outdoor emergency exits benefit from a single SKU family. |
| Glass-Frame Mounting Plate | hotels_motels | `attractive` | 7 | 4 | Basic mechanical alarms (no glass-specific solution) | **superior** | GfS purpose-built mounting plate (Art. 991170) enables installation on glass-framed emergency doors without structural modification. Modern hotel lobby, meeting room, and conference space renovations increasingly use glass-frame doors. Basic mechanical alarm alternatives require custom fabrication or exclude glass-frame doors from monitoring. No equivalent purpose-built accessory exists in the competitor catalog at this price tier. |

**Advantage distribution:** 4 superior · 0 parity · 1 inferior — review note: high superior rate reflects genuine product-category specialization (GfS purpose-built for this function). Inferior verdict on BUS integration is the key competitive gap for S1/S2 hotel segments.

---

sections.title: Key Findings
sections.content:
| Dimension | Finding |
|---|---|
| **Strongest tech features** | Battery-Powered Operation (6.44) and Single-Motion Emergency Release (6.22) — enable the two highest-value hotel outcomes: retrofit deployment without electricians + certified escape compliance |
| **Must-be cluster** | Mechanical Handle Locking + Audible Alarm Trigger + Single-Motion Release + Key Override — four must_be features covering the commodity baseline; no differentiation here |
| **Attractive cluster** | Battery-Powered Operation + Pre-Alarm Warning — above commodity baseline; Battery is the primary enabling feature for hotel ROI case |
| **Top product-related need** | N1/N3 (wireless coverage gaps + battery depletion) — both are direct operational risks that hotel operators will probe during procurement evaluation |
| **Vendor advantage** | Dual-standard coverage + outdoor variant + glass-frame mounting = strong for mixed-hardware hotel portfolios (S1 chains, S3 mid-scale retrofits) |
| **Vendor gap** | BUS Interface integration (inferior vs. dormakaba/GEZE) — decisive blocker for S1 chains and S2 upscale with established BMS; mitigated by C6 gateway but adds cost |
| **Constraint watch** | C6 (EasyWave) × N6 (BMS integration effort) × Wireless BUS inferior verdict — three aligned signals that protocol lock-in is the #1 product-related barrier in the hotel segment |

---

rows.Primary drivers: Safety (9): physically blocks unauthorized door operation — the commodity baseline. Reliability (8): zero-power operation ensures function during hotel power events or fire.
rows.Market evidence: E8 (imp=10, sat=8): escape non-impedance is the baseline expectation; every alternative product delivers it.

rows.Primary drivers: Safety (9): immediate deterrence of propping/misuse. Reliability (7): mechanical piezo trigger needs no power, confirmed by C2 (absolute constraint). Stress (5) deliberately lower: 95 dB alarm on upper floors disturbs guests — reduces experiential score.
rows.Market evidence: E6 (imp=9, sat=4): detecting unauthorized open events is the core need; alarm is the lowest-tech expression of this.

rows.Primary drivers: Safety (10): direct EN 179/1125 evacuation compliance — non-negotiable for any certified product. Skill (8) + Stress (8): unfamiliar hotel guests can escape without prior knowledge per DIN EN 1125 mandate.
rows.Market evidence: E8 (imp=10, sat=8): only satisfied need in the top OS cluster; every certified product delivers this. Confidence (9): operators have documented compliance evidence.

rows.Primary drivers: Stress (7): housekeeping, maintenance, and night audit staff can access silently. Time (6) + Pain (6): eliminates alarm trigger + investigation cycle for routine authorized access.
rows.Market evidence: E7 (imp=8, sat=4): false alert fatigue from authorized staff uses is a top-8 underserved need; key override is the primary mitigation at hardware level.

rows.Primary drivers: Cost (8): eliminates mains wiring conduit — the dominant retrofit cost driver in hotels (S3 mid-scale). Pain (8): removes need for electrical permits, conduit work, and structural disruption. Time (7): enables sub-weekend deployment per door (O4 in outcomes). Delight (7): operators are genuinely surprised that IoT monitoring works without rewiring.
rows.Market evidence: E3 (imp=8, sat=5, product_related=true): commissioning speed is a top-10 underserved need; battery power is the enabler. Not expected — wired (A2) is the traditional paradigm.

rows.Primary drivers: Stress (7): reduces guest disturbance from accidental alarm triggers (housekeeping leaning on door). Pain (6): eliminates investigation burden from handle-touch incidents. Delight (6): unexpected feature that specifically addresses hotel's guest experience sensitivity.
rows.Market evidence: E7 (imp=8, sat=4): false alert reduction is underserved; pre-alarm provides hardware-level filtering before IoT alert layer. Absent from commodity FP and basic variants — above baseline.

rows.Statement: Minimize the likelihood of incomplete wireless coverage from 30 m transmitter range leaving emergency exits on floors above level 3 without active IoT monitoring
Minimize the time required to design and validate wireless receiver placement to achieve >95% floor coverage before hardware procurement in multi-story hotel installations
Minimize the likelihood of 9V battery depletion in high-traffic hotel emergency exits exhausting monitoring capability between scheduled maintenance cycles while mechanical alarm remains functional
Minimize the time required to identify and replace batteries approaching depletion threshold across a 30+ device hotel deployment before wireless monitoring capability is lost
Minimize the likelihood of surface-mounted door guardian housing conflicting with hotel brand interior finish specifications in guest-facing corridors and stairwells
Minimize the effort required to bridge EasyWave event data to hotel BMS platforms running BACnet, KNX, or IP-based protocols without deploying a dedicated GfS gateway per property
Minimize the likelihood of proprietary wireless protocol dependency preventing multi-brand emergency exit monitoring consolidation in hotel portfolio standardization programs
Minimize the likelihood of door-event timestamp and access log data triggering a GDPR Data Protection Impact Assessment that delays IoT system deployment in EU hotel properties

rows.Incumbent Ref: Generic wireless door alarm / Allegion wireless
rows.Rationale: GfS's 868 MHz EU-reserved band provides reliable low-interference transmission in dense hotel RF environments; purpose-built for exit alarm state events. Allegion/Von Duprin wireless monitoring uses higher-frequency BLE/Zigbee with shorter effective range and more interference sensitivity in concrete structures. Constraint C3 (30 m) limits multi-floor hotels but is addressable with repeaters unlike competitors that require per-floor wired drops.

dormakaba SafeRoute natively integrates with LON bus networks and provides centralized software dashboards with open BMS protocols. GEZE SecuLogic supports BACnet/IP directly. GfS BUS Interface is proprietary — hotels with existing BMS (S1 chains, S2 upscale) require additional GfS gateway hardware for protocol translation, adding integration cost and vendor dependency (C6). Full-service hotel chains explicitly require BACnet or KNX native support.

GfS offers matched lever-handle (EN 179, Art. 990140) and panic-bar (EN 1125, Art. 994100) variants from the same product family with shared wireless infrastructure. Hotels have mixed hardware: stairwell ground-floor exits typically require EN 1125 (panic bar per public building code); upper-floor corridor exits use EN 179 lever. dormakaba and GEZE cover both standards but via separate product lines at higher cost/complexity, preventing single-vendor deployment with unified monitoring.

rows.Incumbent Ref: Standard mechanical alarm guards (no published IP rating)
rows.Rationale: GfS IP45 outdoor variant (Art. 990000-IP) enables monitoring of hotel perimeter exits — loading docks, external stairwells, covered parking exits — without a separate product selection. Best mechanical incumbent alternatives (40% of hotel deployments) carry no published IP rating, defaulting to indoor-only installation and excluding outdoor exits from monitoring coverage. Hotels with mixed indoor/outdoor emergency exits benefit from a single SKU family.

GfS purpose-built mounting plate (Art. 991170) enables installation on glass-framed emergency doors without structural modification. Modern hotel lobby, meeting room, and conference space renovations increasingly use glass-frame doors. Basic mechanical alarm alternatives require custom fabrication or exclude glass-frame doors from monitoring. No equivalent purpose-built accessory exists in the competitor catalog at this price tier.

rows.Finding: Battery-Powered Operation (6.44) and Single-Motion Emergency Release (6.22) — enable the two highest-value hotel outcomes: retrofit deployment without electricians + certified escape compliance
Mechanical Handle Locking + Audible Alarm Trigger + Single-Motion Release + Key Override — four must_be features covering the commodity baseline; no differentiation here
Battery-Powered Operation + Pre-Alarm Warning — above commodity baseline; Battery is the primary enabling feature for hotel ROI case
N1/N3 (wireless coverage gaps + battery depletion) — both are direct operational risks that hotel operators will probe during procurement evaluation
Dual-standard coverage + outdoor variant + glass-frame mounting = strong for mixed-hardware hotel portfolios (S1 chains, S3 mid-scale retrofits)
BUS Interface integration (inferior vs. dormakaba/GEZE) — decisive blocker for S1 chains and S2 upscale with established BMS; mitigated by C6 gateway but adds cost
C6 (EasyWave) × N6 (BMS integration effort) × Wireless BUS inferior verdict — three aligned signals that protocol lock-in is the #1 product-related barrier in the hotel segment

entities.rationale: Must_be — every certified product in this commodity delivers physical handle blocking. Primary drivers: Safety (9) blocks unauthorized egress; Reliability (8) zero-power operation survives hotel power events. Stress (7): security officers know exits are mechanically secured. Grounded in E8 (imp=10, sat=8): escape compliance is the commodity baseline expectation.

Must_be — commodity-level baseline; no product variant ships without it. Safety (9): immediate deterrence; Reliability (7): C2-compliant mechanical trigger at 0V. Stress deliberately suppressed to 5: 95 dB alarm audible across hotel floors disturbs guests, reducing experiential value. Grounded in E6 (imp=9, sat=4) — detection is the need; this feature is the minimum-viable form.

Must_be — DIN EN 179/1125 regulatory mandate; no certified product in any hotel can omit this. Safety (10): the single highest score — direct fire evacuation compliance. Skill (8) + Stress (8): unfamiliar hotel guests escape without prior knowledge, per EN 1125 mandate for public buildings. Confidence (9): certification provides documented legal compliance evidence for fire marshal audits. E8 (imp=10, sat=8) is the most-satisfied top need.

Must_be — all standard variants include key override; hotel operators expect silent authorized access capability. Stress (7): housekeeping, maintenance, night audit staff can access without triggering guest-disturbing alarm. Time (6) + Pain (6): eliminates alarm-trigger + investigation cycle for routine operations. Primary market evidence: E7 (imp=8, sat=4) — false alert fatigue from authorized staff uses; key override is the hardware-level mitigation.

Attractive — exceeds commodity FP; wired systems (A2, 20% share) are the traditional paradigm; operators do not expect IoT monitoring without mains wiring. Cost (8): eliminates conduit installation as the dominant retrofit cost driver. Pain (8): removes electrical permits and structural disruption. Delight (7): facility managers are genuinely surprised that IoT monitoring works battery-only. Confidence slightly suppressed (5) due to C4 battery life uncertainty. Grounded in E3 (imp=8, sat=5, product_related=true): commissioning speed is a top-10 need; battery power is the enabler.

Attractive — absent from commodity FP and basic variants; hotels specifically value guest disturbance reduction. Stress (7): reduces guest disruption from accidental handle contact by housekeeping or guests leaning on stairwell doors. Pain (6): eliminates false alarm investigation cycles from incidental touches. Delight (6): unexpected hardware-level false alarm filter. Grounded in E7 (imp=8, sat=4): false alert fatigue is underserved; pre-alarm is the physical-layer mitigation before IoT alert filtering.

entities.statement: Minimize the likelihood of incomplete wireless coverage from 30 m transmitter range leaving emergency exits on floors above level 3 in multi-story hotel installations without active IoT monitoring
Minimize the time required to design and validate wireless receiver placement to achieve >95% floor coverage before hardware procurement in multi-story hotel installations

Minimize the likelihood of 9V battery depletion in high-traffic hotel emergency exits exhausting IoT monitoring capability between scheduled maintenance cycles while the mechanical alarm remains functional
Minimize the time required to identify and replace batteries approaching depletion threshold across a 30+ device hotel deployment before wireless monitoring capability is lost
Minimize the likelihood of surface-mounted door guardian housing conflicting with hotel brand interior finish specifications in guest-facing corridors and stairwells
Minimize the effort required to bridge EasyWave wireless event data to hotel BMS platforms running BACnet, KNX, or IP-based protocols without deploying a dedicated GfS-specific gateway per property
Minimize the likelihood of proprietary wireless protocol dependency preventing multi-brand emergency exit monitoring consolidation in hotel portfolio standardization programs
Minimize the likelihood of door-event timestamp and access log data triggering a GDPR Data Protection Impact Assessment that delays IoT system deployment in EU hotel properties

entities.feature_description: Transmit guard state and alarm events wirelessly to central receivers using 868 MHz EU-reserved low-power band
entities.best_incumbent_technology: Generic wireless door alarm / Allegion wireless exit monitoring
entities.rationale: GfS 868 MHz EU-reserved band provides reliable low-interference transmission in dense hotel concrete structures; purpose-built state machine (guard-open, alarm, battery-low) vs. generic door contact sensors. Allegion wireless exit monitoring uses higher-frequency BLE/Zigbee with shorter effective range and higher interference susceptibility in multi-floor concrete hotels. Vendor limitation: 30 m per hop requires repeater planning in multi-story properties (C3).

entities.feature_description: Aggregate status messages from multiple door guardian units into a centralized monitoring point with external system connectivity
entities.rationale: dormakaba SafeRoute natively integrates with LON bus and provides centralized exit management software with open BMS protocols. GEZE SecuLogic supports BACnet/IP directly. GfS Wireless BUS Interface is proprietary EasyWave — hotels with existing BACnet/KNX BMS (S1 chains, S2 upscale) require an additional GfS-specific gateway for protocol translation, adding cost and long-term vendor dependency. This is the primary competitive gap for hotel BMS integration segments.

entities.feature_description: Cover both EN 179 lever-handle and EN 1125 panic-bar emergency exit door types from a single unified product family with shared wireless infrastructure
entities.rationale: GfS offers EN 179 (Art. 990140) and EN 1125 (Art. 994100) variants from the same product family sharing 868 MHz wireless infrastructure. Hotels have mixed hardware: ground-floor stairwell exits require EN 1125 panic bars (public building code); upper-floor corridor exits use EN 179 levers. dormakaba and GEZE offer both standards but via separate product lines at higher cost, preventing single-vendor unified monitoring. GfS enables hotel-wide standardization from one supplier with one gateway.

entities.feature_description: Operate in outdoor and semi-exposed emergency exit installations with protection against water jets from any direction
entities.best_incumbent_technology: Standard mechanical alarm guards (no published IP rating)
entities.rationale: GfS IP45 variant (Art. 990000-IP) enables monitoring of hotel perimeter exits — loading docks, external stairwells, covered parking garage exits — without a separate product selection. The dominant alternative (mechanical alarms, ~45% share in hotels) carries no published IP rating and defaults to indoor-only installation, leaving outdoor exits unmonitored. Hotels with mixed indoor/outdoor emergency exit portfolios achieve single-SKU-family deployment.

entities.feature_description: Install on glass-framed emergency exit doors without structural modification using a purpose-built surface mounting accessory
entities.best_incumbent_technology: Basic mechanical alarm guards (no glass-specific mounting solution)
entities.rationale: GfS purpose-built mounting plate (Art. 991170) enables installation on glass-frame doors without drilling or structural modification. Contemporary hotel lobby, meeting room, and conference floor renovations increasingly use glass-framed doors. Basic mechanical alarm alternatives require custom fabrication or exclude glass-frame doors from monitoring scope. No purpose-built glass-mounting accessory exists in competitor catalog at this price tier — forced workaround is bespoke metalwork or product exclusion.
